summary: Hotz documents his experience getting AMD to acknowledge and fix critical driver issues affecting the 7900XTX GPU. After public complaints, AMD sent him a private ROCm 5.6 driver tarball that resolved the kernel panics and GPU crashes, but asked him not to distribute it. He dives into AMD's confusing dual-driver architecture (amdgpu vs AMDGPU-Pro) and criticizes their closed development workflow. His core argument is that AMD's cathedral-style closed development undermines their potential competitive advantage against NVIDIA.
key_points: AMD responded to his public complaints with a private driver fix, suggesting they're more concerned with brand perception than with the severity of shipping broken drivers
AMD maintains two driver codebases (amdgpu and AMDGPU-Pro) with confusing overlap, and the public ROCK-Kernel-Driver repo lags behind actual development
The private 5.6 tarball fixed the GPU crashes, but AMD provided no technical details and asked him not to share it
AMD's closed development workflow negates the advantages of having technically open-source drivers
To beat NVIDIA, AMD needs to combine truly open driver development with public hardware documentation
money_quotes: We are hoping that this will improve your perception of AMD products and this will be reflected in your public messaging.

I do sadly feel like what they responded to was george is upset and saying bad things about us which is bad for our brand and not holy shit we have a broken driver released panicing thousands of people's kernels and crashing their GPUs.
My ask to AMD, why keep the real driver development workflow closed source?
Cathedral style is no better than NVIDIA.
And in order to beat them, you must be playing to win, not just playing not to lose.
Combine the driver openness with public hardware docs and you have a competitive advantage.
key_insight: AMD's nominally open-source drivers developed behind closed doors offer no real advantage over NVIDIA's proprietary approach—true openness requires public development workflows and hardware documentation.
